source.title: From the neuromatrix to the pain matrix (and back)

confidence_rationale: Influential theoretical review arguing that pain-matrix activity (including insula) reflects salience/surprise detection rather than pain-specific processing. Directly supports the SD-020 claim that affective harm encoding tracks unexpectedness rather than raw magnitude. Confidence is moderate because it is a review/theoretical position paper rather than direct empirical measurement of PE coding, and does not itself quantify the PE component — that empirical work came in later studies building on this framework.
failure_signatures: If insula responses were shown to correlate purely with nociceptive intensity irrespective of saliency, this framework would be undermined.

mapping.source_claim_statement: The cortical network activated by painful stimuli (the 'pain matrix', including insula, ACC, and frontal areas) is largely unspecific for nociception and instead reflects a salience detection system responding to significant/surprising sensory events regardless of modality.
mapping.ree_translation: If the anterior insula is a salience/surprise detector rather than a raw-intensity encoder, then z_harm_a — which is biologically anchored in AIC — should be trained on surprise (PE) not raw harm accumulation. The SD-020 redesign of the auxiliary loss target directly implements what Iannetti and Mouraux argue the AIC computes.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The salience framework is debated; some researchers (e.g., Legrain et al.) extend it, others contest it. The review does not distinguish anterior from posterior insula in detail, nor does it formalise PE in the precision-weighting sense that SD-020 requires. Transfer from 'salience' to 'precision-weighted PE' needs additional empirical support (provided by Geuter 2017 and Horing 2022).
mapping.source_context: Theoretical synthesis across human EEG, MEG, fMRI, PET studies of acute experimental pain and non-painful comparators

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (>1000 citations, Experimental Brain Research, from leading pain neuroscience labs). Mapping fidelity moderate: the paper argues for salience/surprise but does not use formal PE language or precision-weighting. Transfer risk elevated because the claim is theoretical and the debate about the pain matrix is ongoing; this is strong conceptual support but not direct PE quantification.
